name: Initializing project build command
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The user initially asked about Claude Code commands for building projects after using `/init`, and Claude explained that after running `/init` to generate the CLAUDE.md file, users can simply give natural language instructions like "build the project" rather than specific commands. The conversation then shifted to troubleshooting Python environment issues on a Linux server (gn89) where the user was working on an "org_report" project. The user encountered a pyenv/pip installation problem, which Claude helped resolve by suggesting virtual environment activation. This led to a more complex libffi.so.6 library dependency error when trying to run their Python application, indicating missing system libraries needed for the ctypes module. Since the user lacked sudo privileges and the server had no external network access (unable to download Miniconda), Claude guided them through using existing system libraries via LD_LIBRARY_PATH environment variable configuration. The libffi issue was successfully resolved, but then revealed an ImportError where the main.py file couldn't import the OrgReportDataProcessor class from data_processor.py, suggesting either a naming mismatch or structural issue in the Python code. The user was working in a pyenv virtual environment called "myenv" with Python 3.10.10, and the project files were located in a complex NAS directory structure under their user account.

chat_messages.text:
사용자가 Claude Code에서 `/init` 이후에 프로젝트 코드를 빌드하기 위해 입력할 명령어에 대해 물어보고 있습니다. 

Claude Code는 Anthropic의 명령줄 도구로, 개발자가 터미널에서 직접 Claude에게 코딩 작업을 위임할 수 있게 해줍니다. 

사용자가 Claude Code에 대한 구체적인 질문을 하고 있으므로, 제공된 지침에 따라 https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code의 문서를 확인해야 합니다. 하지만 저는 웹 검색 도구를 사용하여 최신 정보를 확인해야 할 것 같습니다.

그러나 먼저 제 지식을 바탕으로 답변할 수 있는지 생각해봐야 합니다. Claude Code는 비교적 새로운 제품이고, 구체적인 명령어에 대한 질문이므로 공식 문서를 참조하는 것이 좋을 것 같습니다.

지침에 따르면: "Claude tries to check the documentation at https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code before giving any guidance on using this product."

따라서 웹에서 Claude Code 문서를 검색하거나 가져와야 합니다.Claude Code의 공식 문서를 확인해보겠습니다.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

직접 URL을 가져올 수 없으니 웹 검색을 사용해야 합니다.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

좋습니다. 검색 결과를 통해 Claude Code의 `/init` 명령어에 대한 충분한 정보를 얻었습니다. 이제 사용자의 질문에 답변할 수 있습니다.

사용자는 `/init` 이후에 프로젝트 코드를 빌드하기 위해 입력할 명령어를 물어보고 있습니다.

검색 결과에서 확인한 내용:
1. `/init`은 CLAUDE.md 파일을 생성하는 명령어입니다
2. 이 파일은 프로젝트의 빌드, 린트, 테스트 실행 등의 일반적인 명령어를 포함합니다
3. `/init` 이후에는 자연어로 Claude에게 작업을 지시하면 됩니다

따라서 사용자가 원하는 답변은:
- `/init` 후에는 특별한 명령어가 아니라 자연어로 Claude에게 프로젝트를 빌드하도록 요청하면 됩니다
- 예: "프로젝트를 빌드해줘", "npm run build를 실행해줘" 등

한국어로 답변을 작성하겠습니다.Claude Code에서 `/init` 명령어는 프로젝트를 분석하여 CLAUDE.md 파일을 생성하는 명령어입니다. 이 파일에는 빌드, 테스트, 린트 등의 일반적인 명령어 정보가 저장됩니다.

**`/init` 이후 프로젝트 빌드 방법:**

`/init` 실행 후에는 특별한 명령어가 아닌 **자연어로 Claude에게 직접 지시**하면 됩니다:

```
프로젝트를 빌드해줘
```

```
npm run build를 실행해줘
```

```
프로젝트를 빌드하고 테스트도 실행해줘
```

Claude Code는 대화형 인터페이스로, 자연어로 작업을 요청할 수 있습니다. `/init`으로 생성된 CLAUDE.md 파일을 통해 Claude가 프로젝트의 빌드 명령어를 이미 파악하고 있기 때문에, 별도로 정확한 명령어를 입력하지 않아도 자동으로 적절한 빌드 명령어를 실행합니다.

**추가 유용한 명령어:**
- `/clear` - 대화 히스토리 초기화
- `/permissions` - 권한 설정 관리

더 자세한 정보는 [Claude Code 공식 문서](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code)를 참고하시면 좋습니다.

content.title: Cooking with Claude Code: The Complete Guide - Sid Bharath
content.text:
When Claude is done building out the first version of our app, type in /init. This initializes Claude when you use it in a project for the first time. If you have an existing project you want to bring Claude into, run this command first.

First we need to install Claude Code. We’ll install it globally but call it locally. Open up your terminal (Warp.dev is a good one) and run this command anywhere (it’s a global installation so it doesn’t matter where): ... Create your project directory and initialize Claude Code. I have a folder on my Mac called Projects. Inside that I have dozens of folders for different apps and projects. We’re going to create a finance-tracker folder for our app: ... That last command spins up a REPL, a local instance of Claude scoped to that project folder, which means Claude can only see and interact with what’s inside this folder and any sub-folders. The first time you do this, it will walk you through some setup. Fairly straightforward, just follow the instructions. The only thing to watch out is the authentication. You can pay for Claude Code either via API (which is usage based) or connecting an existing Claude account (which you might have if you use the web app a lot).

Claude Code is a CLI (Command Line Interface) that uses Anthropic’s latest models, Sonnet 4 and Opus 4.1, to generate code for you. You give it instructions in your terminal, and the built-in coding agent with its tools executes those commands. If you’ve tried Codex by OpenAI or Jules by Google, it’s kinda like that, but man is it good. Like really, really good. I myself am (or was) a Cursor user for the longest time. And then I tried Claude Code when it came out and haven’t looked back. In this tutorial, we’re going to learn how to use it by building a complete personal finance tracker web app. Along the way, I’ll introduce you to all the features, usage patterns, and tips and tricks to getting the most out of it. Follow along for best results. If you’re a visual learner, here’s a video – ... Hello Claude my old friend, I’ve come to code with you again.

There are tons of other coding agents on the market already, and they all have the same agentic capabilities – they understand codebases, write and edit code, run commands, manage git operations, and coordinate multiple parallel development streams. ... To understand this, it helps to understand how a coding agent works. It’s surprisingly straightforward and I won’t go into detail here but you should read my tutorial on how to build a baby Claude Code from scratch to get an intuition of it. Once you read that, you’ll realize that Claude’s strength comes from its context management and tool calls. In fact, you’ll realize that Claude Code is actually a really well-designed general purpose agent that happens to be good at following a plan and coding. When you’re working on a big feature, it starts with a plan and creates a Todo list. This helps it stay on track and maintains context for the whole session.

Type /permissions to add which commands it can run without asking for permissions · Start claude with the –dangerously-skip-permissions flag to skip all permissions. I would not recommend this unless you’ve set up the advanced documentation and git workflows I describe later. Even on Auto-mode you can stop the process by hitting Esc if you think it’s going off course. The third and final mode is Plan Mode. Instead of jumping straight into code, Claude engages its extended thinking capabilities to create comprehensive strategies. Use Plan Mode when you’re about to start a new feature, tackle a complex challenge, refactor code, or basically any new project. You can control the depth of analysis with specific phrases like “think”, “think hard”, and “ultrathink” for increasing depth in analysis. ... Hey Claude! I want to build a personal finance tracker web app.

content.title: Build your own /init command like Claude Code - Kaushik Gopal's Website
content.text:
Build your own /init command Claude’s /init makes it easy to add clear repo instructions. Build your own and use it with any agent to add or improve on an existing AGENTS.md Here’s the one I came up with. Claude Code really nailed the onboarding experience for agentic coding.

Include the necessary commands to develop in this codebase, such as how to run a single test. 2. High-level code architecture and structure so that future instances can be productive more quickly. Focus on the "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand Usage notes: - If there's already a CLAUDE.md, suggest improvements to it. - When you make the initial CLAUDE.md, do not repeat yourself and do not include obvious instructions like "Provide helpful error messages to users", "Write unit tests for all new utilities", "Never include sensitive information (API keys, tokens) in code or commits" - Avoid listing every component or file structure that can be easily discovered - Don't include generic development practices - If there are Cursor rules (in .cursor/rules/ or .cursorrules) or Copilot rules (in .github/copilot-instructions.md), make sure to include the important parts.

I use a custom /initialize on new repos to get up and running fast2. I tweaked it to work across different coding agents and sprinkled in a few tips I collected along the way. It should create a relevant AGENTS.md; if one exists, it updates it. Save this prompt as a custom command and use it with any tool — Gemini CLI, Codex, Amp, Firebender, etc. You aren’t stuck with any single tool. One more tip: a reasoning model works best for these types of commands. I must say: the more time I spend with these tools, the more “emperor‑has‑no‑clothes” moments I have. Some of the ways these things work are deceptively simple. Claude does a few other things, like instructing its inner agent tools (BatchTool & GlobTool) to collect related files and existing instructions (package*.json, *.md, .cursor/rules/**, .github/copilot-instructions.md, etc.) as context for generating or updating CLAUDE.md.

Build your own and use it with any agent to add or improve on an existing AGENTS.md Here’s the one I came up with. Claude Code really nailed the onboarding experience for agentic coding. Open it, type /init, and you get a CLAUDE.md that delivers better results than a repo without proper system instructions (or an AGENTS.md). It’s a clever way to ramp a repo fast. As I wrote last time, it hits one of the three levers for successful AI coding - seeding the right context. Even Codex CLI now comes with a built-in init prompt. There’s no secret1 sauce: /init is just a strong prompt that writes (or improves) an instructions file. Here’s the prompt, per folks who’ve reverse‑engineered it: Please analyze this codebase and create a CLAUDE.md file, which will be given to future instances of Claude Code to operate in this repository. What to add: 1. Commands that will be commonly used, such as how to build, lint, and run tests.

content.title: Claude Code Best Practices \ Anthropic
content.text:
Tell Claude to pull data via bash commands, MCP tools, or custom slash commands · Ask Claude to read files or fetch URLs (works for images too) Most sessions involve a combination of these approaches. For example, you can pipe in a log file, then tell Claude to use a tool to pull in additional context to debug the logs. Claude Code includes headless mode for non-interactive contexts like CI, pre-commit hooks, build scripts, and automation. Use the -p flag with a prompt to enable headless mode, and --output-format stream-json for streaming JSON output. Note that headless mode does not persist between sessions. You have to trigger it each session. Headless mode can power automations triggered by GitHub events, such as when a new issue is created in your repository. For example, the public Claude Code repository uses Claude to inspect new issues as they come in and assign appropriate labels.

This can reduce performance and sometimes distract Claude. Use the /clear command frequently between tasks to reset the context window. For large tasks with multiple steps or requiring exhaustive solutions—like code migrations, fixing numerous lint errors, or running complex build scripts—improve performance by having Claude use a Markdown file (or even a GitHub issue!) as a checklist and working scratchpad: For example, to fix a large number of lint issues, you can do the following: Tell Claude to run the lint command and write all resulting errors (with filenames and line numbers) to a Markdown checklist · Instruct Claude to address each issue one by one, fixing and verifying before checking it off and moving to the next · Several methods exist for providing data to Claude: Copy and paste directly into your prompt (most common approach) Pipe into Claude Code (e.g., cat foo.txt | claude), particularly useful for logs, CSVs, and large data ·

Tell Claude to pull data via bash commands, MCP tools, or custom slash commands · Ask Claude to read files or fetch URLs (works for images too) Most sessions involve a combination of these approaches. For example, you can pipe in a log file, then tell Claude to use a tool to pull in additional context to debug the logs. Claude Code includes headless mode for non-interactive contexts like CI, pre-commit hooks, build scripts, and automation. Use the -p flag with a prompt to enable headless mode, and --output-format stream-json for streaming JSON output. Note that headless mode does not persist between sessions. You have to trigger it each session. Headless mode can power automations triggered by GitHub events, such as when a new issue is created in your repository. For example, the public Claude Code repository uses Claude to inspect new issues as they come in and assign appropriate labels.

Call claude -p “<your prompt>” --json | your_command, where your_command is the next step of your processing pipeline · That’s it! JSON output (optional) can help provide structure for easier automated processing. For both of these use cases, it can be helpful to use the --verbose flag for debugging the Claude invocation. We generally recommend turning verbose mode off in production for cleaner output. What are your tips and best practices for working with Claude Code? Tag @AnthropicAI so we can see what you're building! Written by Boris Cherny. This work draws upon best practices from across the broader Claude Code user community, whose creative approaches and workflows continue to inspire us. Special thanks also to Daisy Hollman, Ashwin Bhat, Cat Wu, Sid Bidasaria, Cal Rueb, Nodir Turakulov, Barry Zhang, Drew Hodun and many other Anthropic engineers whose valuable insights and practical experience with Claude Code helped shape these recommendations.

A blog post covering tips and tricks that have proven effective for using Claude Code across various codebases, languages, and environments.

content.title: How I Use Claude Code to Build Faster as a Backend Engineer
content.text:
One of the coolest things I discovered ... was the /init command. When I'm working in a new folder or want to set up an existing project to work better with Claude Code, I type /init and let it do its thing. Instead of giving me some generic template, /init looks around my project first. It checks out my files, folder setup, and existing code...

I use /init on every new project now. One thing that really opened up new possibilities for me was learning that Claude Code works with MCPs (Model Context Protocols). This basically means I can connect Claude Code to all sorts of external tools and services. The setup is pretty straightforward. Claude Code can act as both an MCP client (connecting to other services) and an MCP server (letting other tools connect to it). This means I can build some really powerful workflows. One of my favorite setups is connecting Claude Code to Canva through an MCP. Now, when I need social media images for my blog posts, I can ask Claude to create them directly in Canva following my Canva project's flow. Instead of switching between apps and manually designing graphics, Claude handles the whole process. It's amazing for keeping my content creation workflow smooth.

There are tons of MCP servers out there now, and more companies are building them all the time. It's like having a whole ecosystem of tools that Claude Code can tap into whenever it needs them. I'm not going to sugarcoat it … Claude Code can get expensive if you're paying per token. In just three days of heavy use, I burned through about $80 worth of tokens. The pricing is by usage (most expensive), Max 20x ($200), Max 5x ($100) and Pro ($17). I can’t image the Pro gets you very much usage as I sometimes run out of Opus within an hour with Max. The cost is the most common complaint I hear about Claude Code, and it's totally valid. The pricing makes way more sense when you compare it to what you're actually getting. It's using Claude Sonnet (or Opus), which is a much more powerful model that can understand entire codebases and reason through complex problems.

When I stack it up against other AI coding tools, the value becomes clearer. Sure, other models and editors are cheaper for autocomplete, but Claude Code can take in my entire project's context and make *correct* improvements. I use *correct* because just like any other model, it can hallucinate. The game changer for me was upgrading to Claude Max at $100/month. Suddenly, Claude Code usage is bundled in, and I don't have to worry about token costs anymore. There's also a $200 plan with even higher limits that lets you use Opus longer. After doing the math on my usage patterns, the $100 plan pays for itself quickly. I'll probably upgrade to the $200/m MAX in the near future. After 30 days of using Claude Code daily, I can honestly say it's changed how I approach development. Sure, it's not perfect and the pricing can sting, but the productivity gains are real.

Claude Code has changed my workflow

content.title: Init Commands · ruvnet/claude-flow Wiki · GitHub
content.text:
## 🔍 VERIFICATION-FIRST DEVELOPMENT ... failure detection and rollback · # Initialize with Pair Programming mode npx claude-flow@alpha init --pair # Creates CLAUDE.md with: # - Real-time verification workflow # - Collaborative ...

# Initialize with both verification and pair programming npx claude-flow@alpha init --verify --pair # Combines all features: # - Truth Verification System (95% threshold) # - Pair Programming real-time validation # - Background task monitoring # - Complete audit trail # - Multi-agent verification # - Collaborative development workflow · The combined mode creates the most comprehensive configuration: ## 🔍👥 VERIFICATION + PAIR PROGRAMMING ### Workflow Integration 1. Developer writes code (pair mode) 2. AI reviews in real-time (pair mode) 3. Truth verification runs (verify mode) 4. Score must exceed 95% (verify mode) 5. Auto-rollback if failed (verify mode) 6. Suggestions provided (pair mode) 7. Audit trail recorded (verify mode) ### Background Execution All verification commands use run_in_background: - Continuous compilation checking - Real-time test execution - Live linting and formatting - Performance monitoring - Security scanning ·

The Claude-Flow init command system provides specialized configurations for different development workflows: Standard: Basic swarm orchestration and concurrent execution · Verification: Truth enforcement with 95% accuracy threshold ... Choose the appropriate init mode based on your project requirements, quality standards, and collaboration needs. All modes integrate with background commands for efficient asynchronous execution and monitoring. The init command system ensures your project starts with the optimal configuration for your specific development workflow, whether you prioritize verification, collaboration, or advanced AI capabilities.

## 🔍 VERIFICATION-FIRST DEVELOPMENT ### Truth Thresholds - Production: 0.95 (95% accuracy required) - Development: 0.85 (85% accuracy required) - Testing: 0.75 (75% accuracy required) ### Verification Requirements by Agent Type - **Coder**: compile, test, lint, typecheck - **Reviewer**: code-analysis, security-scan, performance-check - **Tester**: unit-tests, integration-tests, coverage-check - **Planner**: task-decomposition, dependency-check, feasibility ### Background Monitoring - Uses run_in_background parameter for continuous validation - Real-time truth score tracking - Automatic failure detection and rollback · # Initialize with Pair Programming mode npx claude-flow@alpha init --pair # Creates CLAUDE.md with: # - Real-time verification workflow # - Collaborative intelligence patterns # - Continuous monitoring setup # - Auto-suggestion system # - Performance tracking metrics # - Interactive feedback loops ...

# Initialize with enhanced features npx claude-flow@alpha init --enhanced # Includes: # - Advanced swarm orchestration # - Neural network integration # - Performance optimization # - Extended agent types # - Custom workflow patterns # - GitHub integration · The enhanced mode includes experimental and advanced features: ## ⚡ ENHANCED CONFIGURATION ### Advanced Features - 27+ neural models - WASM SIMD acceleration - Distributed consensus - Meta-learning capabilities - Adaptive topology switching - Cross-session memory persistence · All init commands create a CLAUDE.md with these core sections: ...

content.title: How I use Claude Code (+ my best tips)
content.text:
Claude Code supports custom hooks, slash commands, and project-specific configuration. The cool part? You can have Claude build these for you. I asked Claude to add a couple default hooks, commands, and settings. It looked at my project and created a settings file that I can easily edit, with a few notable highlights: It added a CLAUDE.md file, which gives a bit of project overview and some key commands that it should know about. This prevents it from having to figure that out each time and scan the codebase for "is there a build command or a lint command?" It always has awareness of that. It adds some hooks for what code should run before edits are accepted, such as run Prettier on a specific file, or after edits, like write a type check on a specific file to make sure that it only accepts good and correct files.

Claude Code supports custom hooks, slash commands, and project-specific configuration. The cool part? You can have Claude build these for you. I asked Claude to add a couple default hooks, commands, and settings. It looked at my project and created a settings file that I can easily edit, with a few notable highlights: It added a CLAUDE.md file, which gives a bit of project overview and some key commands that it should know about. This prevents it from having to figure that out each time and scan the codebase for "is there a build command or a lint command?" It always has awareness of that. It adds some hooks for what code should run before edits are accepted, such as run Prettier on a specific file, or after edits, like write a type check on a specific file to make sure that it only accepts good and correct files.

I switched from Cursor's agents to Claude Code weeks ago and I'm not going back. Here's how I use it and my best practical tips

Claude Code definitely has more features than OpenAI's Codex, though their agents are quite similar. Codex has one huge benefit over Claude Code which has made it a daily drive for me. Read my full comparison of Claude Code vs Codex for the deep dive on how they compare. The terminal interface isn't always ideal. Sometimes you just want to click and highlight text like a normal person. That's where our Builder.io extension comes in. You can launch a visual interface from your IDE that works exactly like Claude Code under the hood. We reverse-engineered it as closely as possible. You get a normal chat interface, live preview, and you can even switch to a Figma-style design mode to make visual edits. Your whole team can use it — designers, PMs, whoever. They can create prototypes, clean up UIs, and submit PRs without needing to understand the terminal interface.

First things first: install the Claude Code extension. It works with VS Code, Cursor, and probably Windsurf too. Don't expect fireworks, it's basically just a launcher. But it makes opening Claude Code dead simple, and you can run multiple instances in parallel in different panes in your IDE as long as they're working on different parts of your codebase. I still use Cursor for quick Command+K completions and tab completions. But the agent sidebar? I only touch it when Claude is down. The weird thing is how my workflow has evolved. I used to have Claude as a small sidebar while coding in the main editor. Now I default to Claude first and only peek at code when reviewing changes. It's become my primary interface, not my secondary one. Yeah, I was skeptical too. A terminal interface for chat-based code editing? Sounds like a step backward. But Anthropic did a decent job with it.

content.title: 20 Claude Code CLI Commands to Make Your 10x Productive
content.text:
The /init command is the key to unlocking Claude's full potential on your projects. It creates a special file that Claude uses to learn about your codebase. ... What it does: Creates a CLAUDE.md file in your project's root directory.

We'll go beyond a simple list of commands and explore the workflows and techniques that can genuinely 10x your productivity. Whether you're a seasoned command-line veteran or just starting to explore the power of AI in your development process, this article will provide you with the knowledge and tools you need to master the Claude Code CLI and become a more effective and efficient developer. ... Want a great API Testing tool that generates beautiful API Documentation? Want an integrated, All-in-One platform for your Developer Team to work together with maximum productivity? Apidog delivers all your demands, and replaces Postman at a much more affordable price! ... Before you can unleash the full power of Claude, you need to get it set up and learn the basics of session management. This section will guide you through the initial steps and introduce you to the fundamental commands for starting, stopping, and resuming your conversations with Claude.

The /init command is the key to unlocking Claude's full potential on your projects. It creates a special file that Claude uses to learn about your codebase. ... What it does: Creates a CLAUDE.md file in your project's root directory. Productivity Boost: The CLAUDE.md file is where you can store high-level information about your project, such as its architecture, dependencies, and coding conventions. Claude will read this file to provide you with more context-aware and accurate assistance. Think of it as giving Claude a "memory" for your project. You can even ask Claude to help you write it with prompts like > document the project architecture in CLAUDE.md. Sometimes, you need to change direction within a session without starting a completely new one. The /clear command is perfect for this. ... What it does: Resets the conversation history and context of your current session.

In the era of Claude Code, Cursor and Loveable AI, the landscape of software development is undergoing a seismic shift. The traditional image of a developer hunched over a keyboard, manually typing every line of code, is rapidly being replaced by a more collaborative and intelligent workflow. At the heart of this revolution is the rise of AI-powered coding assistants, and Anthropic's Claude Code CLI stands out as a particularly powerful tool for developers who live in the terminal. While graphi

What it does: This command installs the Claude Code CLI globally on your system, which means you can run the claude command from any directory in your terminal. Productivity Boost: A one-time setup that gives you instant access to your AI coding assistant from anywhere on your machine. No need to switch to a browser or a separate application; Claude is always just a command away. Small customizations can have a big impact on your workflow. This command allows you to set a preference for how Claude notifies you when it has completed a task. ... What it does: This tells Claude to play your terminal's bell sound when a long-running task is complete. Productivity Boost: Instead of constantly checking your terminal to see if Claude has finished, you can switch to another task and be notified when it's time to review the results. This is a simple yet effective way to maintain focus and multitask efficiently.

content.title: /init in Claude Code 1st or AFTER project activation? · oraios/serena · Discussion #374
content.text:
<command-message>init is analyzing your codebase…</command-message> <command-name>/init</command-name> Please analyze this codebase and create a CLAUDE.md file, which will be given to future instances of Claude Code to operate in this repository. What to add: 1. Commands that will be commonly used, such as how to build, lint, and run tests. Include the necessary commands to develop in this codebase, such as how to run a single test. 2. High-level code architecture and structure so that future instances can be productive more quickly. Focus on the "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand Usage notes: - If there's already a CLAUDE.md, suggest improvements to it.

/init in Claude Code 1st or AFTER project activation?

<command-message>init is analyzing your codebase…</command-message> <command-name>/init</command-name> Please analyze this codebase and create a CLAUDE.md file, which will be given to future instances of Claude Code to operate in this repository. What to add: 1. Commands that will be commonly used, such as how to build, lint, and run tests. Include the necessary commands to develop in this codebase, such as how to run a single test. 2. High-level code architecture and structure so that future instances can be productive more quickly. Focus on the "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand Usage notes: - If there's already a CLAUDE.md, suggest improvements to it.

- When you make the initial CLAUDE.md, do not repeat yourself and do not include obvious instructions like "Provide helpful error messages to users", "Write unit tests for all new utilities", "Never include sensitive information (API keys, tokens) in code or commits" - Avoid listing every component or file structure that can be easily discovered - Don't include generic development practices - If there are Cursor rules (in .cursor/rules/ or .cursorrules) or Copilot rules (in .github/copilot-instructions.md), make sure to include the important parts. - If there is a README.md, make sure to include the important parts. - Do not make up information such as "Common Development Tasks", "Tips for Development", "Support and Documentation" unless this is expressly included in other files that you read. - Be sure to prefix the file with the following text: ``` # CLAUDE.md This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

content.text:
To get started, I recommend creating the basic Claude Code configuration using the /init command. # Navigate to your project root cd your-project # Initialize Claude Code claude # Generate project documentation /init · This creates a CLAUDE.md ...

Step-by-Step Guide: Prepare Your Codebase for Claude Code This comprehensive guide will show you how to set up Claude Code to understand your complete project in just three steps, maximizing its …

This comprehensive guide will show you how to set up Claude Code to understand your complete project in just three steps, maximizing its…

Here you’ll find Agents, Commands, MCPs, Templates, and you can also create your own custom Claude Code execution workflows. Important: I recommend installing only what’s necessary for each project! If Claude Code has too many options, it will start to lose focus, which is exactly what we want to avoid. Deep Graph is an MCP that provides Claude Code with advanced understanding tools for your complete codebase. It adds 6 new tools to Claude Code so it can read code in a much more advanced way, perform semantic searches and node-based searches. This enormously strengthens Claude’s total contextual understanding capabilities of your project. To use it, we need to integrate it through Claude Code’s MCP integration, and for that we’ll need the following data: ... Once you’ve uploaded your repository to CodeGPT, you’ll be able to obtain the CODEGPT_GRAPH_ID.

Run this command with your API Key, YOUR_CODEGPT_API_KEY, CODEGPT_ORG_ID, and CODEGPT_GRAPH_ID: claude mcp add "Deep-Graph-MCP" npx -- -y mcp-code-graph@latest YOUR_CODEGPT_API_KEY CODEGPT_ORG_ID CODEGPT_GRAPH_ID · Pro tip: You can name the MCP after your repository to make it easier for Claude Code to understand which repository this MCP represents. For example: claude mcp add "My-repo-MCP" npx -- -y mcp-code-graph@latest YOUR_CODEGPT_API_KEY CODEGPT_ORG_ID CODEGPT_GRAPH_ID · You can also see complete instructions for connecting the MCP when loading the Code Graph within CodeGPT. ... Note: Read the full tutorial to fork a public repository and use it as your own: https://help.codegpt.co/en/articles/9912447-code-graphs · If you’re going to connect private repositories, you can go to Connections to add Git Providers. You can connect private repositories from Github, Gitlab, Gitlab On Premise, and Bitbucket: ...

content.title: What is Claude Code And How To Use Claude Code (also Strenghts, Weakness and Pricing) | Simplified
content.text:
If you’re not a developer, you might be unfamiliar with using the command line interface, but you can still use natural language to interact with Claude Code in the terminal, similar to vibe coding tools like Replit. ... Build something new from scratch. Give Claude access to your existing codebase for context. In both cases, Claude needs a place to save and access information — a project directory on your local machine. If you have existing code, such as from a vibe coding project, you can clone it from GitHub: git clone <your-project-repo-url> cd my-project claude ... Clones the GitHub repository into a folder. Changes the current directory to the project folder. Starts Claude Code with access to that directory. Bonus: Claude vs ChatGPT: Which AI Assistant Performs Better? Once Claude Code has something to work from, you can begin interacting with it. ... Run the /init command to have Claude Code create a project-specific memory document in your project directory.

For larger projects, /init generates a CLAUDE.md file, which serves as Claude’s memory context. The command reviews your existing project files and summarizes the most relevant details. You can modify this file after it’s created. Typically, you’ll only use /init when you first load a project into Claude Code or after making significant changes to your codebase to refresh the context. Claude automatically refers to CLAUDE.md during your interactions. If needed, you can manually edit it in any Markdown-friendly code editor, such as Cursor. At this stage, you can start giving Claude Code specific instructions. From experience (and as Anthropic also suggests), the following prompt styles tend to work best: Investigate the problem – Example: “Read the file upload handling code and any related error logs. Don’t write any code yet—just explain how file uploads currently work based on what you find.” ·

While you still approve terminal commands, the interaction style remains conversational—similar to vibe coding. Git can get complex when managing multiple branches. Claude Code can help manage these workflows naturally through prompts. ... Accidentally deleted a file during refactoring. Realized the mistake two commits later. Claude Code restored the file in under a minute. Bonus: 15+ No Code Marketing Tools To Help You Create Content Profitably · While Claude Code can handle big projects, performance can vary when the context window is heavily loaded. It may overlook certain files or dependencies if they fall outside its processing limit. For extensive codebases, you might need to guide it toward specific files or components. If your initial prompt isn’t explicit, Claude Code might start writing code immediately—even when you only want analysis.

Learn what is Claude Code, how to use it, its strengths, weaknesses, and pricing to decide if it’s the right tool for your development needs.

검색 결과에서 확인한 내용:
1. `/init`은 CLAUDE.md 파일을 생성하는 명령어입니다
2. 이 파일은 프로젝트의 빌드, 린트, 테스트 실행 등의 일반적인 명령어를 포함합니다
3. `/init` 이후에는 자연어로 Claude에게 작업을 지시하면 됩니다

따라서 사용자가 원하는 답변은:
- `/init` 후에는 특별한 명령어가 아니라 자연어로 Claude에게 프로젝트를 빌드하도록 요청하면 됩니다
- 예: "프로젝트를 빌드해줘", "npm run build를 실행해줘" 등

summaries.summary: 검색 결과를 종합하여 자연어 명령 방식 설명했습니다.

content.text:
Claude Code에서 `/init` 명령어는 프로젝트를 분석하여 CLAUDE.md 파일을 생성하는 명령어입니다. 이 파일에는 빌드, 테스트, 린트 등의 일반적인 명령어 정보가 저장됩니다.

**`/init` 이후 프로젝트 빌드 방법:**

`/init` 실행 후에는 특별한 명령어가 아닌 **자연어로 Claude에게 직접 지시**하면 됩니다:

```
프로젝트를 빌드해줘
```

```
npm run build를 실행해줘
```

```
프로젝트를 빌드하고 테스트도 실행해줘
```

Claude Code는 대화형 인터페이스로, 자연어로 작업을 요청할 수 있습니다. `/init`으로 생성된 CLAUDE.md 파일을 통해 Claude가 프로젝트의 빌드 명령어를 이미 파악하고 있기 때문에, 별도로 정확한 명령어를 입력하지 않아도 자동으로 적절한 빌드 명령어를 실행합니다.

**추가 유용한 명령어:**
- `/clear` - 대화 히스토리 초기화
- `/permissions` - 권한 설정 관리

더 자세한 정보는 [Claude Code 공식 문서](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code)를 참고하시면 좋습니다.

chat_messages.text:
사용자가 pyenv를 사용하는 환경에서 pip 명령어를 찾을 수 없다는 에러를 받고 있습니다. 에러 메시지를 보면:
- pip 명령어가 여러 Python 버전에 존재함 (3.10.10, 3.10.10/envs/myenv, myenv)
- 현재 활성화된 Python 환경이 없거나 pip이 없는 버전을 사용 중

해결 방법:
1. pyenv로 사용할 Python 버전 설정
2. 또는 가상환경 활성화
3. 직접 python -m pip 사용

한국어로 답변해야 합니다.pyenv 환경에서 pip을 찾을 수 없다는 에러입니다. 현재 활성화된 Python 환경이 pip을 포함하지 않는 것으로 보입니다.

**해결 방법:**

## 1. 가상환경 활성화 (권장)
```bash
pyenv activate myenv
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

## 2. 또는 전역 Python 버전 설정
```bash
pyenv global 3.10.10
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

## 3. 또는 로컬 디렉토리용 Python 버전 설정
```bash
pyenv local 3.10.10
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

## 4. python -m pip 직접 사용
```bash
python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
```

**현재 상태 확인:**
```bash
# 현재 활성화된 Python 버전 확인
pyenv version

# 사용 가능한 Python 버전 목록
pyenv versions

# 현재 Python 경로 확인
which python
```

**가장 좋은 방법:**
프로젝트 작업을 위해서는 **가상환경 활성화**가 가장 좋습니다:
```bash
pyenv activate myenv
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

어떤 방법을 시도하셨는지 알려주시면 더 구체적으로 도와드릴 수 있습니다!

chat_messages.text:
이 오류는 `libffi.so.6` 공유 라이브러리가 없어서 발생하는 문제입니다. 이는 Python의 ctypes 모듈이 필요로 하는 시스템 라이브러리인데, 시스템에 설치되지 않았거나 Python이 찾을 수 없는 상황입니다.

해결 방법은:
1. 시스템에 libffi를 설치
2. LD_LIBRARY_PATH 환경변수 설정
3. 또는 Python을 재설치 (libffi가 포함되도록)

사용자가 리눅스 시스템(gn89 서버)을 사용하고 있는 것 같으므로, sudo 권한이 있는지에 따라 해결 방법이 달라집니다.`libffi.so.6` 공유 라이브러리가 없어서 발생하는 오류입니다. 이는 Python의 ctypes 모듈이 필요로 하는 시스템 라이브러리입니다.

**해결 방법:**

## 1. libffi 라이브러리 찾기
```bash
# 시스템에 libffi가 설치되어 있는지 확인
find /usr -name "libffi.so*" 2>/dev/null
```

## 2. libffi가 있다면 심볼릭 링크 생성 (sudo 권한 필요)
```bash
# libffi.so.7이나 libffi.so.8이 있다면
sudo ln -s /usr/lib64/libffi.so.8 /usr/lib64/libffi.so.6
# 또는
sudo ln -s /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libffi.so.8 /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libffi.so.6
```

## 3. sudo 권한이 없다면: LD_LIBRARY_PATH 설정
```bash
# libffi 경로 확인 후
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib64:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
python main.py
```

## 4. 또는 conda를 사용한 새 환경 생성 (권장)
```bash
# pyenv 대신 conda 사용
conda create -n org_report python=3.10
conda activate org_report
pip install -r requirements.txt
python main.py
```

## 5. 시스템 관리자에게 libffi 설치 요청 (sudo 권한 필요)
```bash
# CentOS/RHEL
sudo yum install libffi-devel

# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt-get install libffi-dev
```

**빠른 임시 해결책:**
```bash
# 현재 시스템의 libffi 버전 확인
ls -la /usr/lib64/libffi.so*

# 있는 버전으로 환경변수 설정
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib64:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
python main.py
```

서버 환경인 것 같은데, sudo 권한이 있으신가요? 그에 따라 최적의 해결 방법을 알려드리겠습니다.

content.thinking:
이 오류는 `libffi.so.6` 공유 라이브러리가 없어서 발생하는 문제입니다. 이는 Python의 ctypes 모듈이 필요로 하는 시스템 라이브러리인데, 시스템에 설치되지 않았거나 Python이 찾을 수 없는 상황입니다.

해결 방법은:
1. 시스템에 libffi를 설치
2. LD_LIBRARY_PATH 환경변수 설정
3. 또는 Python을 재설치 (libffi가 포함되도록)

사용자가 리눅스 시스템(gn89 서버)을 사용하고 있는 것 같으므로, sudo 권한이 있는지에 따라 해결 방법이 달라집니다.

summaries.summary: Thinking about resolving missing libffi library dependency in Python environment
libffi 라이브러리 설치 방법 모색 중
Diagnosed missing library dependency and outlined installation solutions.

content.text:
`libffi.so.6` 공유 라이브러리가 없어서 발생하는 오류입니다. 이는 Python의 ctypes 모듈이 필요로 하는 시스템 라이브러리입니다.

**해결 방법:**

## 1. libffi 라이브러리 찾기
```bash
# 시스템에 libffi가 설치되어 있는지 확인
find /usr -name "libffi.so*" 2>/dev/null
```

## 2. libffi가 있다면 심볼릭 링크 생성 (sudo 권한 필요)
```bash
# libffi.so.7이나 libffi.so.8이 있다면
sudo ln -s /usr/lib64/libffi.so.8 /usr/lib64/libffi.so.6
# 또는
sudo ln -s /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libffi.so.8 /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libffi.so.6
```

## 3. sudo 권한이 없다면: LD_LIBRARY_PATH 설정
```bash
# libffi 경로 확인 후
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib64:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
python main.py
```

## 4. 또는 conda를 사용한 새 환경 생성 (권장)
```bash
# pyenv 대신 conda 사용
conda create -n org_report python=3.10
conda activate org_report
pip install -r requirements.txt
python main.py
```

## 5. 시스템 관리자에게 libffi 설치 요청 (sudo 권한 필요)
```bash
# CentOS/RHEL
sudo yum install libffi-devel

# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt-get install libffi-dev
```

**빠른 임시 해결책:**
```bash
# 현재 시스템의 libffi 버전 확인
ls -la /usr/lib64/libffi.so*

# 있는 버전으로 환경변수 설정
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib64:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
python main.py
```

서버 환경인 것 같은데, sudo 권한이 있으신가요? 그에 따라 최적의 해결 방법을 알려드리겠습니다.
SUDO권한 없음

chat_messages.text:
sudo 권한이 없는 상황이네요. 이 경우 시스템 라이브러리를 직접 설치할 수 없으므로, 다른 방법을 사용해야 합니다.

가능한 해결책:
1. 기존 libffi.so.7 또는 libffi.so.8을 찾아서 LD_LIBRARY_PATH로 연결
2. Python을 다시 빌드하거나 다른 Python 버전 사용
3. conda 사용 (conda는 자체 libffi를 포함)
4. pyenv로 Python을 다시 설치하되 libffi를 포함하도록

sudo 권한 없이 가장 효과적인 방법은 conda를 사용하는 것입니다. conda는 Python과 함께 필요한 모든 시스템 라이브러리를 포함하고 있어서 이런 문제를 피할 수 있습니다.sudo 권한이 없다면, 다음 방법들을 시도해보세요:

## 1. 시스템의 libffi 찾아서 환경변수 설정 (가장 빠름)
```bash
# libffi 찾기
find /usr -name "libffi.so*" 2>/dev/null

# 찾은 경로에 libffi.so.7 또는 .8이 있다면
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib64:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
# 또는
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH

python main.py
```

영구 적용하려면:
```bash
echo 'export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib64:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

## 2. Conda 사용 (권장 - 가장 확실한 방법)
```bash
# Miniconda 설치 (sudo 권한 불필요)
wget https://repo.anaconda.com/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
bash Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -b -p $HOME/miniconda3

# conda 초기화
$HOME/miniconda3/bin/conda init bash
source ~/.bashrc

# 새 환경 생성
conda create -n org_report python=3.10
conda activate org_report
pip install -r requirements.txt
python main.py
```

## 3. pyenv로 Python 재설치 (libffi 포함)
```bash
# 현재 환경 삭제
pyenv uninstall myenv

# Python 재설치 (빌드 시 libffi 포함하도록)
CONFIGURE_OPTS="--enable-shared" pyenv install 3.10.10

# 새 가상환경 생성
pyenv virtualenv 3.10.10 myenv
pyenv activate myenv
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

**추천 순서:**
1. **먼저 방법 1 시도** (가장 빠름)
2. 안 되면 **방법 2 (conda)** - 가장 확실함
3. 마지막으로 방법 3

방법 1을 시도해보시고 결과를 알려주시면 다음 단계를 안내해드리겠습니다!

chat_messages.text:
네트워크 연결이 안 되는 서버인 것 같습니다. 이 경우 외부 다운로드가 불가능하므로 시스템에 이미 설치된 라이브러리를 활용해야 합니다.

방법 1 (LD_LIBRARY_PATH 설정)을 시도해야 합니다.네트워크 접속이 안 되는 서버네요. 외부 다운로드 없이 해결해야 합니다.

## 해결 방법: 시스템의 기존 libffi 사용

**1단계: libffi 찾기**
```bash
find /usr -name "libffi.so*" 2>/dev/null
# 또는
find /lib* /usr/lib* -name "libffi.so*" 2>/dev/null
```

**2단계: 찾은 결과에 따라 처리**

### A. libffi.so.7 또는 libffi.so.8이 있는 경우:
```bash
# 예: /usr/lib64/libffi.so.8 을 찾았다면
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib64:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH

# 테스트
python main.py
```

### B. 찾은 게 없다면 전체 lib 경로 추가:
```bash
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib64:/usr/lib:/lib64:/lib:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
python main.py
```

**3단계: 성공하면 영구 설정**
```bash
echo 'export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib64:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

**또는 pyenv Python 재빌드 (네트워크 불필요)**
```bash
pyenv uninstall myenv
PYTHON_CONFIGURE_OPTS="--enable-shared" pyenv install 3.10.10
pyenv virtualenv 3.10.10 myenv
pyenv activate myenv
pip install -r requirements.txt
python main.py
```

먼저 `find` 명령어로 libffi를 찾아보시고 결과를 알려주세요!
